You are a SEALED implementer under the barrier protocol at /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/PROTOCOL.md — read it first and comply strictly.
ACCESS RULES: You must NOT read any file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/crates/, and NOT read any Haskell source of tamarin-prover anywhere on disk (notably /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/tamarin-prover/{lib,src}/ and /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/tamarin-prover-testing/{lib,src}/). Do NOT read /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/INTEGRATION_REPORT.md, and do NOT read any AUDIT.md anywhere in the cleanroom. No web access. You MAY run the oracle /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/wellformedness/oracle/wf_oracle.sh and read .spthy examples under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/tamarin-prover/examples/. No git commits.
DISCIPLINE: log every oracle interaction in workspace QUERIES.log (Session 7); record behavioral facts in BEHAVIOR.md with provenance. Oracle scripts carry OOM guards — preserve them. Return a PLAIN-TEXT summary.

Workspace: /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/wellformedness/ (read/write freely inside it, except AUDIT.md). Crate: workspace/wf-clean.

THE TASK — round 7, one observable your rounds never gated on: the batch run's trailing summary prints "WARNING: <N> wellformedness check failed!" where N = the NUMBER OF INDIVIDUAL FINDINGS in the report list your check_theory returns (the caller takes the list length). Your wf BLOCK is byte-perfect corpus-wide, but for topics where you bundle several findings into ONE returned entry the count under-reports. Five corpus files are wrong (your count → expected): features/auto-sources/tamarin-repo/sapic/statVerifLeftRight/stateverif_left_right.spthy → 3; regression/trace/issue515.spthy → 14 (its block: "Reserved names" carries most findings, "Special facts" carries 2 — test2 lhs and test2 rhs are separate findings); regression/trace/issue527.spthy → 14; sapic/deprecated/accountability-old/CertificateTransparency.spthy → 5; sapic/deprecated/accountability-old/OCSPS.spthy → 6.
The footer count is DIRECTLY OBSERVABLE: wf_oracle.sh prints the full batch output including the trailing summary — probe it (constructed theories with known finding multiplicities per topic) to pin exactly what counts as one finding for every topic you emit (per rule? per numbered item? per variable group?), including the grouped-list topics (Reserved names, Unbound variables, Special facts, the public-constants/fact-capitalization/fact-arity topics, the lhs-not-rhs list). Then restructure check_theory's return so entries are per-finding at exactly that granularity, while every rendered BLOCK stays byte-identical (your render layer may need to group same-topic entries — it must, since the block output is already correct).
ACCEPTANCE: all existing tests green UNCHANGED in output terms (block fixtures byte-identical, corpus5 pin silent, round5/6 fixtures pass); new tests asserting the entry COUNT for probe fixtures and for the five corpus files' finding multisets; BEHAVIOR.md records the per-topic counting law with probe provenance. Do NOT edit anything outside /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/wellformedness/.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# Clean-room reimplementation protocol — tamarin-rs relicensing
2	
3	Purpose: independently recreate three modules of tamarin-rs (wellformedness
4	checker, graph/dot pipeline, web layer) without access to GPL-covered
5	expression, so the results are original works relicensable at will.
6	
7	## Rooms
8	- DIRTY ROOM (Kevin Milner + main-session assistant): has read the GPL Haskell
9	  source and the ported Rust modules. Produces only: interoperability headers
10	  (type surfaces, entry-point signatures, comments stripped), oracle wrappers,
11	  and behavioral bug reports. Never contributes implementation text.
12	- CLEAN ROOM (isolated agents, one per cluster): receive ONLY the materials
13	  listed per cluster below. Their complete tool-call transcripts are retained
14	  as evidence of non-access.
15	
16	## Clean-room materials (exhaustive)
17	1. This protocol, the cluster SPEC.md, and interface/ headers (type surfaces +
18	   boundary signatures only).
19	2. Black-box oracles: `wf_oracle.sh` (runs the compiled Haskell binary on an
20	   input file), `hs_server.sh` (serves a theory in the Haskell web UI for HTTP
21	   probing), pre-captured oracle outputs (oracle/captures/,
22	   oracle/captured_responses/), and GPL example .spthy files used solely as
23	   observation INPUTS.
24	3. Public Rust toolchain and general documentation.
25	
26	## Forbidden to the clean room
27	- Reading ANY file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/ — source, comments, git
28	  history — with the sole exceptions of executing the oracle scripts and
29	  reading oracle/captured_responses/ (captured program OUTPUT, not source).
30	- Fetching tamarin-prover source, docs describing its internals, or its GitHub.
31	- Reproducing any tamarin-prover implementation detail from model memory:
32	  every behavior must be derived from the oracle, the captures, or the SPEC.
33	  Exact output strings (warning texts, DOT syntax, HTML) MUST be taken from
34	  observed oracle output — that is compatibility content, not expression.
35	
36	## Audit & integration (after implementation)
37	1. Transcript audit: verify from the retained agent transcripts that no
38	   forbidden path was accessed.
39	2. Similarity audit: a reviewer (may see both sides) compares the clean code
40	   against the Haskell originals; flagged similarities are sent back as
41	   redo-instructions, never patched directly.
42	3. Behavioral acceptance: the FULL-CORPUS gate for the slice must pass, not a
43	   fixture subset (see "Full-corpus gates" below). A narrow fixture suite that
44	   passes while the corpus gate fails is not acceptance.
45	4. Integration: the open side may write thin adapters and mechanical renames to
46	   fit the workspace API, but must not transplant logic from the replaced
47	   modules. The replaced modules are deleted; their GPL-pending headers go
48	   with them (history retains them under GPL). Every integrator that touches a
49	   slice with a full-corpus gate MUST run that gate before declaring green.
50	
51	## Full-corpus gates (standard, not optional)
52	
53	Each slice has a fast oracle gate over the whole 419-file example corpus,
54	diffing the slice's output against the Haskell reference cache. These are the
55	sealed implementer's primary data source (run between build and the HS cache
56	every iteration) AND the integrator's mandatory acceptance check. Both take
57	`RS_PATH` (point at any build) and `ALLOWLIST` (subset while iterating).
58	
59	- Wellformedness: `scripts/wf_gate.sh` — runs the binary WITHOUT `--prove`
60	  (the wf block prints at theory-load, ~1s/file; full corpus ~72s at JOBS=6),
61	  extracts the `/* WARNING … */` block, diffs vs `scripts/.hs_file_cache`.
62	- Web: `scripts/web_parity.sh` — crawls HS and RS interactive servers per
63	  theory, semantic-diffs the response manifests vs `scripts/.web_hs_cache`.
64	- Full batch (proof output, slow ~30-60min): `scripts/corpus_file_diff.sh` —
65	  the ground-truth `--prove` byte diff; run at milestones, not every iteration.
66	
67	Once a slice's ported code is DELETED, the integrated module IS the sealed
68	reimplementation, so running the gate against the tree observes the sealed
69	code's own output vs the reference — legitimate feedback, not observation of
70	replaced code. For a not-yet-integrated slice the tree still holds the ported
71	code, so gate only the workspace build (via `RS_PATH`), never the stock tree.
72

1	# Clean-room task: wellformedness checker
2	
3	Build a standalone Rust crate `wf-clean` in workspace/ that reimplements,
4	from BLACK-BOX BEHAVIOR ONLY, the wellformedness checking of the
5	tamarin-prover binary (the oracle).
6	
7	## What the oracle does
8	`oracle/wf_oracle.sh <file.spthy>` prints the tool's full output for a
9	security-protocol theory file. Within that output, wellformedness checking
10	produces either `/* All wellformedness checks were successful. */` or a
11	WARNING section listing check failures grouped under underlined topic
12	headers. Your job: given a parsed theory (data model in
13	interface/ast_types.rs), produce byte-identical report text and the
14	structured API in interface/required_api.md.

16	## Method requirements
17	- Derive the set of checks, their trigger conditions, their exact message

19	  oracle: write minimal .spthy inputs that trip each check one at a time.

21	  starting points for mutations) in oracle/examples/. The .spthy language
22	  syntax may be learned from those examples and the oracle's parse errors.

27	- Tests: for each check, a fixture .spthy + expected report snippet, asserted
28	  against your implementation AND spot-verified against the oracle.
29	- Dependencies: std only. You may NOT parse .spthy yourself; your crate's
30	  input is the ast_types.rs data model (write test values directly as Rust
31	  constructors; oracle fixtures verify message/ordering behavior).

1	//! wf-clean: a clean-room reimplementation of the tamarin-prover wellformedness
2	//! checker, derived purely from black-box oracle behavior. See
3	//! workspace/BEHAVIOR.md for the inferred behavioral spec.
4	
5	pub mod ast;
6	pub mod checks;
7	pub mod formula;
8	pub mod pretty;
9	pub mod report;
10	
11	pub use ast::*;
12	pub use report::{
13	    insert_wf_before, render_report, topics, underline_topic, WfError, WfReport, SUCCESS_LINE,
14	};
15	
16	/// Topics that follow the public-names report in the canonical order; the
17	/// public-names report is inserted immediately before the first of these that
18	/// is present in the report. (Unbound variables and Fresh public constants
19	/// precede the insertion point and are therefore NOT anchors.)
20	pub fn after_public_names_topics() -> Vec<&'static str> {
21	    vec![
22	        checks::T_SORTS,
23	        checks::T_RESERVED,
24	        checks::T_RESERVED_PREFIX,
25	        checks::T_FR,
26	        checks::T_SPECIAL,
27	        checks::T_FACT_CAP,
28	        checks::T_ARITY,
29	        checks::T_MULT,
30	        checks::T_LHSRHS,
31	        checks::T_LEFT,
32	        checks::T_RIGHT,
33	        checks::T_QUANT_SORTS,
34	        checks::T_FORMULA_TERMS,
35	        checks::T_GUARD,
36	        checks::T_LEMMA_ANNOT,
37	        checks::T_MULRESTRICT,
38	        checks::T_NAT,
39	        checks::T_SUBTERM,
40	    ]
41	}
42	
43	/// Run every check in the oracle's report order. The "Formula terms" check runs
44	/// in free-variable-only mode; for reducible-function coverage use
45	/// [`check_theory_with_reducible`].
46	pub fn check_theory(thy: &Theory) -> WfReport {
47	    check_theory_with_reducible(thy, &std::collections::BTreeSet::new())
48	}
49	
50	/// Run every check in the oracle's report order, passing a caller-computed set
51	/// of reducible function-symbol names to the "Formula terms" check (theories
52	/// with `equations:` make some user functions reducible; the caller derives
53	/// that set - typically from the equation theory / Maude - and this pipeline
54	/// only consumes it).
55	pub fn check_theory_with_reducible(
56	    thy: &Theory,
57	    reducible: &std::collections::BTreeSet<String>,
58	) -> WfReport {
59	    let mut report: WfReport = Vec::new();
60	    // Base pipeline (public names inserted afterwards at its anchor point).
61	    report.extend(checks::unbound_variables(thy)); // 1
62	    report.extend(checks::fresh_public_constants(thy)); // 2
63	    report.extend(checks::mismatching_sorts(thy)); // 4
64	    report.extend(checks::reserved_names(thy)); // 5
65	    report.extend(checks::reserved_prefixes(thy)); // 6 (diff mode only)
66	    report.extend(checks::fr_facts(thy)); // 7
67	    report.extend(checks::special_facts(thy)); // 8
68	    report.extend(checks::fact_capitalization(thy)); // 8b (precedes arity)
69	    report.extend(checks::fact_arity(thy)); // 9
70	    report.extend(checks::fact_multiplicity(thy)); // 10
71	    report.extend(checks::fact_lhs_occur_no_rhs(thy)); // 11
72	    report.extend(checks::diff_left_right(thy)); // 12, 13 (diff mode only)
73	    // The Quantifier-sorts / Formula-terms / Formula-guardedness bundle is
74	    // emitted per formula item (lemmas then restrictions), interleaved by topic.
75	    report.extend(checks::formula_reports(thy, reducible)); // 14, 15, 16
76	    report.extend(checks::lemma_annotations(thy)); // 17
77	    report.extend(checks::multiplication_restriction(thy)); // 17
78	    report.extend(checks::nat_sorts(thy)); // 18
79	    report.extend(checks::subterm_convergence(thy)); // 19
80	
81	    let pn = checks::public_names_report(thy); // 3
82	    let anchors = after_public_names_topics();
83	    insert_wf_before(&mut report, pn, &anchors);
84	    report
85	}
86	
87	/// Secondary entry point: report any of `lemma_names` that are not declared in
88	/// the theory. NOTE: this entry point's exact render string could not be
89	/// observed through the file oracle; the set-difference logic is verified but
90	/// the message text is a documented gap (see BEHAVIOR.md).
91	pub fn check_if_lemmas_in_theory(lemma_names: &[String], thy: &Theory) -> WfReport {
92	    let present = checks::theory_lemma_names(thy);
93	    let mut out = Vec::new();
94	    for name in lemma_names {
95	        if !present.contains(name) {
96	            out.push(WfError::new(
97	                "Lemmas",
98	                format!("lemma `{}' referenced but not present in theory", name),
99	            ));
100	        }
101	    }
102	    out
103	}
104	
105	// Re-export the public secondary entry points named in required_api.md.
106	pub use checks::{
107	    fact_lhs_occur_no_rhs, formula_terms, formula_terms_reducible, public_names_report,
108	    public_names_report_from_pairs,
109	};

1	# Behavioral spec inferred from the oracle (wellformedness checker)
2	
3	All facts below are derived from oracle observation (probes in
4	scratchpad/probes, captures in oracle/captures). Nothing here is taken from
5	tamarin-prover source.
6	
7	## Overall report shape
8	
9	The oracle emits the wellformedness result inside the pretty-printed theory,
10	wrapped in a block comment.
11	
12	Success (empty report):
13	
14	    /* All wellformedness checks were successful. */
15	
16	Failure (>=1 topic):
17	
18	    /*
19	    WARNING: the following wellformedness checks failed!
20	
21	    <block1>
22	
23	    <block2>
24	    ...
25	    */
26	
27	Inner text (what `render_report` produces for a non-empty report) is:
28	
29	    "WARNING: the following wellformedness checks failed!" + "\n\n" + join(blocks, "\n\n")
30	
31	Each `block` = topic header + "\n" + underline + "\n\n" + body, where
32	`underline` = '=' repeated char_count(topic) (topic length INCLUDING any
33	leading/trailing space). Bodies may themselves contain blank lines.
34	
35	`render_report` for the empty report returns the success line
36	`/* All wellformedness checks were successful. */`.
37	`underline_topic(t)` returns `t + "\n" + "=".repeat(char_count(t))`.
38	
39	## Canonical topic order (report order)
40	
41	Observed by kitchen-sink probes ks1 / ks3 and the round-2 ordering probes
42	(comb1, ord_tail, mega_tail, diff_mega, diff_place):
43	
44	 1. `Unbound variables`
45	 2. `Fresh public constants`                             (round2; always-on)
46	 3. `Public constants with mismatching capitalization`   (public-names report)
47	 4. `Variable with mismatching sorts or capitalization`
48	 5. `Reserved names`
49	 5a. `Reserved prefixes`                                 (round2; DIFF MODE ONLY)
50	 6. `Fr facts must only use a fresh- or a msg-variable`
51	 7. `Special facts`
52	 7b. `Fact capitalization issues`                        (round3; before arity)
53	 8. `Fact arity issues`                                  (non-diff; diff merges these
54	 9. `Fact multiplicity issues`                            two into "Fact usage" - OUT OF SCOPE)
55	10. `Facts occur in the left-hand-side but not in any right-hand-side ` (TRAILING space)
56	10a. `Left rule` / `Right rule`                          (round2; DIFF MODE ONLY)
57	10b. `Quantifier sorts`                                  (round4; per-item bundle)
58	11. `Formula terms`
59	12. ` Formula guardedness`   (LEADING space)
60	    NOTE (round4): topics 10b/11/12 are NOT globally fixed - they are emitted
61	    per formula item (see "Formula-check bundle" below); their relative order
62	    depends on which item emits which topic first.
63	13. `Lemma annotations`                                  (round2; always-on)
64	14. `Multiplication restriction of rules`                (round2; always-on)
65	15. `Nat Sorts`
66	16. `Subterm Convergence Warning`
67	17. `Message Derivation Checks` (appended by binary; needs Maude - OUT OF SCOPE)
68	    / `Derivation Checks` (timeout variant - OUT OF SCOPE)
69	
70	Round-2 ordering evidence: comb1 -> `Unbound, Fresh public constants,
71	Public names, mismatching sorts, Reserved`; mega_tail -> `Formula terms,
72	Formula guardedness, Lemma annotations, Multiplication restriction, Nat Sorts,
73	Subterm`; diff_mega/diff_place -> `... Reserved names, Reserved prefixes,
74	Fr facts, ... lhs-not-rhs, Left/Right rule, Formula terms, ...`.
75	
76	Public names (#3) is generated separately and *inserted before* the first of a
77	set of anchor topics = every topic from #4 (mismatching sorts) onward.
78	`after_public_names_topics()` returns that anchor list; `insert_wf_before(
79	report, errs, anchors)` inserts `errs` immediately before the first report
80	entry whose topic is in `anchors` (appends at end if none present). Unbound
81	variables (#1) and Fresh public constants (#2) are emitted before the
82	public-names insertion point (so they are NOT anchors).
83	
84	### Diff-mode-only vs. always-on
85	
86	Gated on `theory.is_diff`: `Reserved prefixes`, `Left rule`, `Right rule`
87	(observed silent in non-diff mode; diff_reserved_prefix without `--diff` emits
88	nothing). Always-on (both modes): `Fresh public constants`, `Lemma
89	annotations`, `Multiplication restriction of rules`.
90	
91	## Per-check triggers and message templates
92	
93	### Unbound variables
94	Trigger: a protocol rule uses a variable in actions/conclusions not bound by a
95	premise. Entries joined by "\n  \n":
96	    "  rule `NAME' has unbound variables: \n    v1, v2"
97	(trailing space after "variables:"). Vars sorted, ", " joined, term-printed.
98	
99	### Public constants with mismatching capitalization (public_names_report)
100	Trigger: two public constants whose names are equal under ASCII-lowercasing but
101	differ in capitalization. Body:
102	    "Identifiers are case-sensitive, mismatched capitalizations are considered as different, i.e., 'ID' is different from 'id'. Check the capitalization of your identifiers.\n\n" + numbered items
103	Each lowercased-key group -> one item "  N. " + occurrences. ROUND5 (issue527
104	target): each DISTINCT spelling is attributed to the rule of its FIRST
105	occurrence only ('second' used in four rules lists just `rule "One"`); the
106	spellings are ASCII-sorted and consecutive spellings sharing their first rule
107	merge into one segment: `rule "R1":  name 'Alice', 'alice'`; segments joined
108	", ": `rule "Four":  name 'firSt', rule "Two":  name 'first'` (the segment
109	order follows the sorted spellings, not the source rules). Groups are sorted
110	by their lowercased key. `public_names_report_from_pairs` takes raw
111	(name, location) pairs in occurrence order.
112	
113	### Variable with mismatching sorts or capitalization  (round5: index-aware)
114	Trigger: within one rule, two variables share a base name AND numeric index but
115	differ in sort or capitalization. The grouping key is (lowercased name, index):
116	`$x.1` vs `x.2` is SILENT, `$x.1` vs `x.1` reports "1. $x.1, x.1" (probes
117	s5_idx/s5_idxsame/s5_capdiff). Suffix-sort spellings are the same variable as
118	their sigil form (`x:pub` = `$x`, silent - s5_suffix) and render with the
119	sigil (`x:pub` vs `~x` -> "$x, ~x" - s5_suffix2). Fixed header:
120	    "Possible reasons:\n1. Identifiers are case sensitive, i.e.,'x' and 'X' are considered to be different.\n2. The same holds for sorts:, i.e., '$x', 'x', and '~x' are considered to be different.\n"
121	per rule: "  rule `A5': \n    1. $s, ~s" (trailing space; numbered variant
122	groups). Variants are ordered by sort class $ < ~ < msg < % (s5_all4; Node
123	unobserved, placed last) and by exact name within a class ("$Ab, $aB" -
124	s5_capord; sort rank beats name order - s5_crossname "$x, ~X"). Groups are
125	sorted by their key and separated by a 4-space line (s5_groups). Rule entries
126	join with "\n  \n" (issue527 target).
127	
128	### Reserved names  (round5: builtin normalization)
129	Trigger: rule uses a fact with a reserved name in a position. The reserved set
130	is POSITION-DEPENDENT (observed z9/z11/z12):
131	  - left-hand-side / right-hand-side: {K, KU, KD}
132	  - the middle (actions): {K, KU, KD, In, Out, Fr}  (the I/O facts are also
133	    reserved as actions; on LHS/RHS they are "Special facts" instead)
134	BUILTIN NORMALIZATION (round5, t5_ku_lhs/t5_ku_all/t5_kd_mid/t5_up_inout +
135	issue515 target): fact names matching KU/KD/In/Out/Fr CASE-INSENSITIVELY
136	denote the builtin facts and render canonically - `Ku(x)` reports `!KU( x )`
137	(persistent), `Kd` -> `!KD( x )`, `OUT`/`FR` -> `Out( x )`/`Fr( x )`; an `IN`
138	premise is a legal In premise (silent). `K` is single-letter (only the exact
139	spelling parses). Normalized builtins never produce Fact-capitalization
140	conflicts (issue515 has Ku/KU/Kd/KD and no such topic).
141	Per (rule, position) entry joined "\n  \n":
142	    "  Rule `R1' contains facts with reserved names on left-hand-side:\n    <facts>"
143	Position phrases: LHS `on left-hand-side:`, actions `on the middle:`,
144	RHS `on the right-hand-side:`. Facts via fact printer, fsep comma-joined.
145	
146	### Fr facts must only use a fresh- or a msg-variable
147	Trigger: `Fr(t)` where t is not fresh/msg var. Entries joined "\n  \n":
148	    "  rule `R1' fact: Fr( $p )"
149	
150	### Special facts
151	Trigger: reserved I/O fact in disallowed position (Out/Fr/K in premises,
152	In/K in conclusions). Per (rule, side) joined "\n  \n":
153	    "  rule `R1' uses disallowed facts on left-hand-side:\n    Out( x )"
154	(sides `on left-hand-side:`/`on right-hand-side:`; lowercase "rule").
155	
156	### Fact arity issues
157	Trigger: same fact name with >1 arity. Body:
158	    "Same fact is used with different arities, i.e., Fact('A','B') is different from Fact('A'). \nCheck the arguments of your facts.\n  " + factBlocks
159	facts sorted by lowercased name; factBlock (each prefixed "\n"):
160	    "\n  Fact `foo':\n\n" + items + "\n  "
161	item i: "    {i}. {Label} `{owner}', arity {n}\n         {render}"; items joined
162	"\n    \n". Rules and LEMMAS both contribute (interleaved in theory-item order);
163	restrictions do NOT (r3_restrarity). Rule facts: Label=`Rule`, render=fact pp.
164	Lemma action facts: Label=`Lemma`, render = raw Haskell `Fact {factTag =
165	ProtoFact {Linear|Persistent} "{name}" {arity}, factAnnotations = fromList [],
166	factTerms = [{de-Bruijn terms}]}` (r3_lemarity). ROUND5: items dedup per
167	(label, owner, arity, RENDER) - the same lemma fact at two binder depths
168	yields TWO items (`Bound 3,2,1` and `Bound 4,3,2` - t5_lemdup, issue527
169	target). Multiplicity is analogous and ALSO gathers lemma facts (r3_lemmult,
170	item = `... multiplicity (persistence) ...`).
171	
172	### Fact multiplicity issues
173	Same structure keyed on multiplicity; header:
174	    "Same fact is used with different multiplicities, i.e., !Fact() (Persistent fact) exists along with Fact() (Linear) in your rules. \nCheck the multiplicity (persistence) of your facts.\n  " + factBlocks
175	item i: "    {i}. {Label} `{owner}', multiplicity (persistence) {Linear|Persistent}\n         {render}".
176	
177	### Fact capitalization issues (round3; precedes Fact arity issues)
178	Trigger: two facts whose names are equal under ASCII-lowercasing but differ in
179	exact spelling (e.g. `Send` vs `SEND`; facts must start upper-case, so the
180	difference is in non-first letters). Same block shape as Fact arity, header:
181	    "Fact names are case-sensitive, different capitalizations are considered as different facts, i.e., Fact() is different from FAct(). \nCheck the capitalization of your fact names.\n  " + factBlocks
182	Unlike arity, EVERY occurrence is listed (NO per-(rule,cap) dedup - `Send` twice
183	in one rule -> two items; r3_capord). item i:
184	    "    {i}. Rule `{rule}', capitalization \"{exactName}\"\n         {factPP}".
185	Groups keyed by lowercased name, sorted; a group needs >=2 distinct spellings.
186	Gathers rule facts across premises/actions/conclusions (source order).
187	
188	### Facts occur in the left-hand-side but not in any right-hand-side  (fact_lhs_occur_no_rhs)
189	Trigger: user fact identity (name,arity,mult) in some premise, never in any
190	conclusion. Numbered items joined "\n  \n":
191	    "  1. in rule \"r1\":  factName `X' arity: 0 multiplicity: Linear" +
192	      optional ". Perhaps you want to use the fact in rule \"r1\":  factName `Y' arity: 0 multiplicity: Linear"
193	suggestion = nearest RHS fact by edit distance under a threshold (omitted when
194	nothing close). ROUND5: the excluded facts are the normalized builtins
195	{KU,KD,In,Out,Fr} only - the reserved proto-fact `K` PARTICIPATES on both
196	sides (issue527 target lists a K-only premise with suggestion `F`; issue515's
197	K premise is satisfied by a K conclusion). Item numbers are RIGHT-ALIGNED to
198	the widest index with a 2-space margin: "   1." .. "  10." (t5_align; ble and
199	mesh targets).
200	
201	### Fresh public constants (round2; always-on)
202	Trigger: a fresh-name literal (`~'foo'`, AST `Term::FreshLit`) used directly in
203	a rule. Constants collected in the order premises, conclusions, actions
204	(probe fpc_positions). Per rule with >=1 hit, entries joined "\n  \n":
205	    "  rule `NAME': fresh public constants are not allowed: ~'foo', ~'bar'"
206	constants rendered by the term printer. The list is a fillSep at width 69 with a
207	4-space continuation indent, begun after the header `  rule `{name}': fresh
208	public constants are not allowed:` (round3: r3_freshwrap; identical mechanism to
209	the Formula-terms list). CLOSED - was a documented gap.
210	
211	### Reserved prefixes (round2; DIFF MODE ONLY)
212	Trigger (diff theories only): a fact whose name starts with `DiffIntr` or
213	`DiffProto`. Facts collected in the order premises, actions, conclusions
214	(probe rp_multi). Per rule the body is:
215	    "  The Rule `NAME' contains facts with reserved prefixes ('DiffIntr',\n  'DiffProto') inside names:\n  \n" + factblocks
216	The HEADER is word-filled (Wadler `fillSep`) at WIDTH 69, indent 2 (measured via
217	rp_long/rp_med/rp_w47: a line breaks before the next word once column would
218	exceed 69). Words treat the rule name `\`NAME'` and each of `('DiffIntr',` /
219	`'DiffProto')` as single tokens. Each factblock (joined "\n  \n"):
220	    "    <fact_pp>\n    (ProtoFact <Mult> \"<name>\" <arity>,<arity>,<Mult>)"
221	The 2nd line is a raw Haskell Show of a (FactTag, arity, Multiplicity) tuple;
222	<Mult> is `Linear`/`Persistent`. GAP: multi-RULE joining unprobed (fixtures use
223	one rule).
224	
225	### Left rule / Right rule (round2; DIFF MODE ONLY)
226	Trigger (diff theories only): a diff rule (`Rule.left_right = Some((l,r))`)
227	whose explicit `left`/`right` projection differs from the parent rule projected
228	to that side (`diff(a,b)` -> a on left, -> b on right). Per rule the LEFT
229	projection is tested first; if inconsistent the RIGHT is not reported for that
230	rule (probe diff_both: both sides bad -> only "Left rule"). Body per entry
231	(joined "\n  \n"):
232	    "  Inconsistent left rule\n" + indent4(explicit_rule_pp) +
233	    "\n  \n  w.r.t.\n  \n" + indent4(parent_rule_pp)
234	("left"/"right" and topic "Left rule"/"Right rule"). Consistency compared via
235	the projected vs. explicit fact lists. Left rule topic precedes Right rule.
236	
237	### Lemma annotations (round2; always-on)
238	Trigger: a lemma with attribute `reuse` AND quantifier `exists-trace` (reuse on
239	`all-traces` is fine - probe la_alltraces). Entries joined "\n  \n":
240	    "  Lemma `NAME': cannot reuse 'exists-trace' lemmas"
241	(Topic may host other annotation conflicts not yet observed.)
242	
243	### Multiplication restriction of rules (round2; always-on)
244	Trigger: a rule whose CONCLUSIONS contain a multiplication (`*`, AST
245	`BinOp::Mult`) term. `*` only in premises or only in actions does NOT trigger
246	(probes mul_multi, mul_act); a `*` in a premise is not listed even when the
247	conclusion also has one (mul_pc). Per rule, entry (joined "\n  \n"):
248	    "  The following rule is not multiplication restricted:\n" + indent4(rule_pp) +
249	    "\n  \n  After replacing reducible function symbols in lhs with variables:\n" + indent4(rule2_pp) +
250	    "\n  \n    Terms with multiplication:  <terms>"
251	<terms> = maximal `*`-subterms of the conclusions, ", " joined (mul_terms).
252	GAPS: (a) rule2 (LHS reducible symbols replaced by fresh vars) is rendered equal
253	to rule1 - correct only when the LHS has no reducible symbols (probe mul_exp
254	shows `g^c` -> `x.1` otherwise); (b) the alternate failure mode
255	"Variables that occur only in rhs:  <vars>" (mul_exp) is not implemented; (c)
256	the co-emitted `Message Derivation Checks` block is Maude-derived, OUT OF SCOPE;
257	(d) rule_pp wrapping for long rules (see printer note).
258	
259	### Quantifier sorts  (round4)
260	Trigger: a lemma/restriction formula quantifies over a variable whose sort is
261	PUB or FRESH (message/temporal/nat quantifiers are allowed - probes
262	qs_pub_prefix/qs_fresh_prefix/qs_nat_prefix/qs_msg_plain). Per item:
263	    "  {Entity} `{name}' uses quantifiers with wrong sort: {tokens}"
264	Entity = `Lemma`/`Restriction` (both, probe qs_restr). Each offending variable
265	is a raw Haskell tuple token `("{name}",LSort{Pub|Fresh})`, collected in binding
266	order (outer binders before inner), rendered as a fillSep at width 69 with a
267	4-space continuation indent (same mechanism as Formula terms; probe qs_multi).
268	Multiple wrong-sort items join as separate emissions in the bundle (consecutive
269	ones merge with `\n  \n`; probe qs_twolem). This topic is emitted BEFORE Formula
270	terms within a single item (probe syn_pubsuffix).
271	
272	### Formula-variable sorts and sort-aware binding  (round4 - supersedes round2)
273	Formula quantifiers bind SORTED variables. A bound variable and a use are the
274	same variable only when they share a name AND a sort CLASS, so a temporal binder
275	`#x` does NOT bind a message-position use `x` (probe g1_core: `Free x`); a
276	message binder `x` does not bind a node use `#x` (g1_msgnode: `Free #x`). Sort
277	classes: {Msg, Untagged, Suffix(Msg)} collapse to Msg (this keeps a quantified
278	message var bound to its uses across the parser's Untagged-vs-Msg tagging - the
279	round2 false-positive fix, now done sort-aware instead of name-only);
280	Pub/Fresh/Node/Nat (and their Suffix forms) are each their own class. ROUND5:
281	the variable's numeric INDEX is part of its identity too - a binder `y` does
282	not bind a use `y.1` (probe g5_idx: `Ex y #i. A(y.1)@#i` -> `Free y.1` and an
283	unguarded binder; the accountability translation's primed variables `S.1`
284	depend on this). The de Bruijn stack counts EVERY quantified var (temporals
285	and sort-mismatched ones included); the index is the distance to the innermost
286	binder matching (name, index, class) - probe g1_dbsort: `All x #x. ... h(x)`
287	-> `h(Bound 1)` (msg x sits under node #x). Same-name/different-sort binders
288	coexist without capture (g1_samename: `Ex x #x. A(x)@#x` -> SUCCESS).
289	
290	### Formula terms  (round3: FULL coverage; round4: sort-aware)
291	Trigger: a lemma/restriction formula uses a TERM "of the wrong form". The unit
292	checked is each ARGUMENT TERM of each atom (Eq/Less/.. -> both sides; Action ->
293	the TEMPORAL first, then the fact args; Pred/Last -> args). A term is wrong iff
294	it contains a FREE variable OR a REDUCIBLE function symbol; the WHOLE top-level
295	term is then reported (not the offending subterm). Body:
296	    "  {Entity} `{name}' uses terms of the wrong form: {termlist}\n  \n" + fixed_help

322	### Formula guardedness (topic has LEADING space)  (round4: decision procedure)
323	Trigger: a LEMMA formula not convertible to guarded form. (An unguarded
324	RESTRICTION is a FATAL error, not a warning -- observed gr_restr/z1; lemmas
325	only.) Body:
326	    "  Lemma `{name}' cannot be converted to a guarded formula:\n    {reason}\n      \"<sub>\"\n    in the formula\n      \"<whole>\""
327	where <sub> is the FAILING QUANTIFIER SUBTREE and <whole> is the full lemma
328	formula. Exactly two reasons:
329	  - `unguarded variable(s) '<v1>', '<v2>' in the subformula` (vars in binding
330	    order, ", " joined, each `'<pp_var>'`; probes ge_unguard/qs_multi)
331	  - `universal quantifier without toplevel implication`
332	
333	DECISION PROCEDURE (pre-order; first failure reported, antecedent before
334	consequent, left before right - probes gr_both/gr_sib; ROUND5 revision):
335	  - QUANTIFIER FUSION: directly nested quantifiers of the SAME kind fuse into
336	    one binder list BEFORE the check (`∀x.∀#i.φ` = `∀x #i.φ` - g5_a_nest,
337	    `∃x.∃#i.φ` - g5_e_nest; predicate-expanded timepoints.spthy needs this).
338	    The report renders the FUSED form for both the failing subformula and the
339	    whole formula (g5_a_nest_noimpl prints "∀ x #i. A( x ) @ #i"). No
340	    cross-kind fusion (g5_a_fuse_ex errors; g5_e_fuse_all: a ∀ body guards
341	    nothing for the outer ∃).
342	  - `∀ vars. body`: body MUST be a top-level implication `guard ==> rest`;
343	    otherwise "without toplevel implication" (bare atom, conjunction,
344	    disjunction, negation, iff ALL fail this way - gu_bareatom/gu_conj/gu_disj/
345	    gu_neg/gu_iff). If it is `guard ==> rest`, every quantified var must be
346	    RESOLVED by the guard region (= the antecedent; equalities in the
347	    consequent do not guard - g5_a_conseq_eq), else "unguarded variable(s)";
348	    then recurse into guard then rest.
349	  - `∃ vars. body`: every quantified var must be RESOLVED by the guard region
350	    (= the whole body), else "unguarded variable(s)"; then recurse into body.
351	  - GUARD RESOLUTION of a region for quantified vars `vs` (round5 -
352	    supersedes the round-4 action-only guard set):
353	      1. every var occurring anywhere inside an ACTION atom reachable through
354	         CONJUNCTIONS only is resolved (deep args count - g5_e_actdeep; all
355	         action atoms are collected BEFORE the equality pass regardless of
356	         their position - g5_e_actorder);
357	      2. the conjunction-reachable EQUALITY atoms are processed in a SINGLE
358	         left-to-right pass: if one side of an equality contains NO unresolved
359	         quantified variables ("clean" - outer-bound and free vars are clean:
360	         g5_e_eqpair/g5_e_eqfree), all unresolved quantified vars of the OTHER
361	         side (however deep - g5_e_eqinner, whole tuples at once -
362	         g5_e_pairboth) become resolved. One pass only: `(z='c') & (w=h(z))`
363	         succeeds but `(w=h(z)) & (z='c')` leaves w unguarded
364	         (g5_e_eqchain/g5_e_revchain); side-based, not unification
365	         (g5_e_unif); both-sides-current fails (g5_e_eqself/g5_e_eqtwo);
366	         temporal equalities behave identically (g5_e_eqtemp). Equalities can
367	         guard ∀ vars in the antecedent the same way (g5_a_eqg1/g5_a_eqg2/
368	         g5_a_eqonly, and the round-4 gx_eq_ant `x=x` rejection is the
369	         both-sides-current case).
370	    Disjunction, negation, implication, `<`/`⋖`/`⊏`/`last` atoms, and nested
371	    quantifiers contribute NOTHING (gx_disj_ant/gx_neg_ant/gx_less/gx_last/
372	    gx_quant_g/g5_e_disjeq/g5_e_neg_eq/g5_e_lessonly/g5_e_lastonly/
373	    g5_e_subterm/g5_e_conjnest). Matching is (name, INDEX, sort-class)-aware
374	    (g5_idx: a binder `y` does not bind or guard `y.1`).
375	  - `¬`, `∧`, `∨`, `⇒`, `⇔` at the top: recurse into operands (left first).
376	  - Any non-quantified formula: no failure (gt_noquant/gt_topconj).
377	  - CORPUS PIN: the reference accepts all 459 lemma + 225 restriction
378	    formulas of the 71 round-5 gate-diff theories (captures in
379	    scratchpad/corpus5_out, extracted to wf-clean/tests/corpus5); the decision
380	    above accepts every one (test corpus5_acceptance).

420	EQUATION LAYOUT ENGINE (pp_equation; calibrated on t5_tup*/t5_last* and the
421	ble/mesh reference blocks, all byte-exact):
422	  - Every fit check compares an end column against min(100, 67 + N) where N is
423	    the NEST of the current line - the continuation column that started it
424	    (2 for the "  = " line, else the fill column that broke).
425	  - A term is laid FLAT when its flat width fits from the current column.
426	  - Function application `f(a1, a2)`: when broken, args fill at the column
427	    after `f(`; a fitting arg is placed flat with `, ` between args mid-line
428	    but a bare `,` glued at a break (mesh: line ends "n),"); a non-fitting arg
429	    breaks to the continuation column and lays out in full. The closing `)`

The sealed report is byte-identical to the round6 target through the last
sealed topic. Verified by diffing the target (truncated before the Maude
`Message Derivation Checks` block) against `tests/fixtures/t5_issue527.txt`:

## Round 7 — per-topic finding-count law (the batch footer `<N>`)

Provenance: probes `round7/probes/*` and `round7/probes_extra/*` plus the five
whole-file oracle runs (all logged in QUERIES.log under "ROUND 7"). Every
number below is read directly from the oracle's trailing summary line.

### The observable
After proving, `wf_oracle.sh` prints in "summary of summaries":

    WARNING: <N> wellformedness check failed!

`N` is the LENGTH of the report list — the number of `(topic, doc)` pairs the
checker emits — NOT the number of numbered items rendered inside a block. Two
findings can share a topic block (rendered under one underlined header) yet
count as two. Conversely a block that lists many numbered items can be a single
finding (e.g. lhs-not-rhs). The integrated caller computes `N` as the length of
the report list (`check_theory(thy)` entries plus the caller-appended
out-of-scope findings), so `check_theory` must return ONE entry per finding at
exactly this granularity.

### Per-topic granularity (what counts as one finding)

WHOLE-TOPIC — one finding for the entire topic no matter how many groups/items:
  - `Public constants with mismatching capitalization` (pub_1grp/pub_2grp = 1)
  - `Fact capitalization issues` (fcap_1grp/fcap_1grp_3occ/fcap_2grp = 1)
  - `Fact arity issues` (arity_1grp/arity_2grp = 1)
  - `Fact multiplicity issues` (mult_2grp = 1)
  - `Facts occur in the left-hand-side but not in any right-hand-side ` (lhs_1/lhs_2 = 1;
    the block still lists every numbered item)
  - `Subterm Convergence Warning` (subterm_2eq = 1)

PER RULE — one finding per protocol rule that trips the check:
  - `Unbound variables` (deriv_2var one rule/two vars = 1; deriv_2rule = 2)
  - `Variable with mismatching sorts or capitalization` (sort_2grp_1rule = 1;
    sort_2rule = 2). The fixed "Possible reasons:" preamble is a topic heading
    shown once — carried on the FIRST rule's finding.
  - `Fresh public constants` (freshpub_2const_1rule = 1; freshpub_2rule = 2)
  - `Multiplication restriction of rules` (multrestrict_2rule_dh = 2)
  - `Reserved prefixes` (diff only; kept per-rule, unprobed)

PER (rule, side) — one finding per rule×position block:
  - `Reserved names` (reserved_2fact_1side: both facts on the left = 1;
    issue515 = 12 rule-side blocks). Positions: left-hand-side / the middle /
    the right-hand-side.
  - `Special facts` (special_2fact_1side In-on-RHS = 1; issue515 test2 = 2:
    Out on the left + In on the right).
  - `Left rule` / `Right rule` (diff only; per inconsistent rule, unprobed)

PER offending FACT occurrence:
  - `Fr facts must only use a fresh- or a msg-variable` (fr_2fact_1rule = 2)

PER FORMULA ITEM (each lemma/restriction the check visits):
  - `Quantifier sorts` (qs_2lem = 2)
  - `Formula terms` (ft_2lem = 2; the several free-var occurrences inside one
    lemma stay inside that item's single finding)

PER LEMMA:
  - ` Formula guardedness` (guard_2lem = 2)
  - `Lemma annotations` (lemanno_2lem = 2)

`Nat Sorts` was not probed for count; it is emitted per distinct entry (same
